name: Vocational interests (RIASEC)
source: Holland's RIASEC model — John L. Holland, Making Vocational Choices (1973). Activity-rating format modeled on the public-domain O*NET Interest Profiler (U.S. Department of Labor). Items here are original statements in that public-domain style; no proprietary instrument's items are reproduced.

dimensions.name: Realistic
dimensions.description: Hands-on, practical, physical work with tools, machines, or the outdoors.

dimensions.name: Investigative
dimensions.description: Analyzing, researching, solving abstract or scientific problems.

dimensions.name: Artistic
dimensions.description: Creating, designing, expressing; unstructured, original work.

dimensions.name: Social
dimensions.description: Helping, teaching, advising, working closely with people.

dimensions.name: Enterprising
dimensions.description: Leading, persuading, selling, starting and running things.

dimensions.name: Conventional
dimensions.description: Organizing, following procedures, working with data and detail.

items.text: Repair or build something mechanical or electronic.
Set up and operate machines or equipment.
Work outdoors or with your hands for most of the day.
Assemble a physical prototype from parts.
Diagnose why a device or system stopped working.
Investigate the root cause of a hard, ambiguous problem.
Design an experiment to test an idea.
Analyze data to find a pattern others missed.
Read deeply about how something works, for its own sake.
Model or simulate a system before building it.
Create an original design, story, or piece of art.
Work on open-ended problems with no single right answer.
Improvise a new approach rather than follow a template.
Craft how something looks, sounds, or reads.
Express an idea in an unconventional format.
Teach or explain something to help someone learn.
Coach or mentor someone toward a goal.
Resolve a disagreement between people.
Spend the day working closely alongside others.
Support someone through a difficult situation.
Persuade people to adopt your idea or plan.
Lead a team toward an ambitious target.
Start something new and own its outcome.
Negotiate a deal or pitch to decision-makers.
Take a calculated risk for a bigger payoff.
Organize information into a clear, reliable system.
Follow a precise procedure to get things exactly right.
Keep detailed records accurate and up to date.
Check work carefully for errors and inconsistencies.
Build a repeatable process for a recurring task.
